contentBody: Neha's Python script turns on the AC at 26C and lights after 7 PM. Which AI paradigm does her script best represent?
answerExplanation: The script consists entirely of explicit if-else conditions written by a human, making it Rule-based AI. Supervised ML learns patterns from given data; this script uses pre-written explicit logic. Unsupervised learning finds hidden patterns without explicit labels; this script has explicit rules. Reinforcement learning does not apply here; the script does not learn from experience.

contentBody: A teacher wants to predict if a student will Pass or Fail based on attendance, study hours, and mock score. Which column is the label (y)?
answerExplanation: The label (y) is the target column the model is trying to predict, which is the Pass/Fail result. Attendance, study hours, and mock score are input features (X) used to find patterns and make the prediction.

contentBody: Priya has 5,000 rows. She does a `test_size=0.20` split, then splits the rest with `test_size=0.25`. How many rows will the validation set have?
answerExplanation: The first split leaves 4,000 rows. Twenty-five percent of those 4,000 rows equals exactly 1,000 validation rows. Five hundred would only be 10% of the original dataset. Seven hundred fifty does not represent the correct mathematical split. One thousand two hundred fifty would be 25% of the original 5,000, but the second split happens on the remaining 4,000.

contentBody: Rohit's DummyClassifier gets 68% accuracy on a test set where 68% of labels are "Pass". What best explains this result?
option.1: The model learned patterns from 68% of training data
option.2: The model correctly identifies difficult test edge cases
option.3: It always predicts "Pass" and reflects class distribution
option.4: The model was evaluated on the 68% of training data
answerExplanation: The dummy model simply predicts the majority class ("Pass") every single time without learning anything. A DummyClassifier does not learn patterns from the training features. The baseline dummy model does not evaluate complex edge cases. The model was evaluated on the test set, but it achieved 68% simply because the test set contains 68% "Pass" labels.

contentBody: Zara runs Python ML code on her basic budget laptop using Google Colab and the output appears instantly. Why is it so fast?
option.1: Her laptop has a hidden Python turbo-boost feature
option.2: Colab executes code on Google's cloud servers, not her laptop
option.3: Colab compresses code before running it on any machine
option.4: Python automatically optimises itself on budget laptops
answerExplanation: Google Colab provides a remote runtime, meaning the heavy computation executes on Google's cloud servers. Laptops do not possess a hidden Python turbo-boost feature. Code compression is not the mechanism that makes Google Colab fast. Python does not automatically optimize hardware performance on budget laptops.
